paperTitle: Beyond the Assistant Turn: User Turn Generation as a Probe of Interaction Awareness in Language Models
abstract: Standard LLM benchmarks evaluate the assistant turn: the model generates a response to an input, a verifier scores correctness, and the analysis ends. This paradigm leaves unmeasured whether the LLM encodes any awareness of what follows the assistant response. We propose user-turn generation as a probe of this gap: given a conversation context of user query and assistant response, we let a model generate under the user role. If the model's weights encode interaction awareness, the generated user turn will be a grounded follow-up that reacts to the preceding context. Through experiments across $11$ open-weight LLMs (Qwen3.5, gpt-oss, GLM) and $5$ datasets (math reasoning, instruction following, conversation), we show that interaction awareness is decoupled from task accuracy. In particular, within the Qwen3.5 family, GSM8K accuracy scales from $41\%$ ($0.8$B) to $96.8\%$ ($397$B-A$17$B), yet genuine follow-up rates under deterministic generation remain near zero. In contrast, higher temperature sampling reveals interaction awareness is latent with follow up rates reaching $22\%$. Controlled perturbations validate that the proposed probe measures a real property of the model, and collaboration-oriented post-training on Qwen3.5-2B demonstrates an increase in follow-up rates. Our results show that user-turn generation captures a dimension of LLM behavior, interaction awareness, that is unexplored and invisible with current assistant-only benchmarks.

chunks.text: Standard LLM benchmarks evaluate the assistant turn: the model generates a response to an input, a verifier scores correctness, and the analysis ends. This paradigm leaves unmeasured whether the LLM encodes any awareness of what follows the assistant response. We propose user-turn generation as a probe of this gap: given a conversation context of user query and assistant response, we let a model generate under the user role. If the model's weights encode interaction awareness, the generated user turn will be a grounded follow-up that reacts to the preceding context. Through experiments across $11$ open-weight LLMs (Qwen3.5, gpt-oss, GLM) and $5$ datasets (math reasoning, instruction following, conversation), we show that interaction awareness is decoupled from task accuracy. In particular, within the Qwen3.5 family, GSM8K accuracy scales from $41\%$ ($0.8$B) to $96.8\%$ ($397$B-A$17$B), yet genuine follow-up rates under deterministic generation remain near zero. In contrast, higher temperature sampling reveals interaction awareness is latent with follow up rates reaching $22\%$. Controlled perturbations validate that the proposed probe measures a real property of the model, and collaboration-oriented post-training on Qwen3.5-2B demonstrates an increase in follow-up rates. Our results show that user-turn generation captures a dimension of LLM behavior, interaction awareness, that is unexplored and invisible with current assistant-only benchmarks.

Beyond the Assistant Turn: User Turn Generation as a Probe
                                        of Interaction Awareness in Language Models

Sarath Shekkizhar∗, Romain Cosentino, Adam Earle

Abstract

Standard LLM benchmarks evaluate the assistant turn: the model gener-
                                                  ates a response to an input, a verifier scores correctness, and the analysis
                                                  ends. This paradigm leaves unmeasured whether the LLM encodes any
arXiv:2604.02315v1 [cs.AI] 2 Apr 2026

awareness of what follows the assistant response. We propose user-turn
                                                  generation as a probe of this gap: given a conversation context of user query
                                                  and assistant response, we let a model generate under the user role. If the
                                                  model’s weights encode interaction awareness, the generated user turn will
                                                  be a grounded follow-up that reacts to the preceding context. Through

1     Introduction

Large language models (LLMs) are post-trained on token sequences that span both sides
                                        of a conversation, i.e., the user and assistant (Qwen Team, 2026; OpenAI, 2025; Zeng et al.,
                                        2025). Yet, standard evaluation benchmarks focus only on the response generated under the
                                        assistant role (Cobbe et al., 2021; Rein et al., 2024; Pyatkin et al., 2025). Consequently, a model
                                        can score 96% on GSM8K and yet, when made to continue under the user role, completely
                                        fall apart. Failures show up as generating text from a previous turn or continuing as the
                                        assistant, a case of identity drift (Shekkizhar et al., 2025). The assistant response is correct;

Figure 1: User-turn generation as a probe of interaction awareness. Left: A conversation
context consisting of system, user, and assistant turns delimited by special tokens (e.g.,
<|im start|>, <|im end|>). Standard evaluation scores the assistant response for task accuracy.
Our probe appends a <|im start|>user header and lets model Mθ generate under the user
role. A genuine follow-up indicates the model’s weights encode conversational awareness.
Right: Genuine follow-up rate (%) vs. sampling temperature for six models across three
datasets. At greedy decoding (T =0), most models produce near-zero follow-ups. Increasing
temperature surfaces latent interaction awareness in Qwen3.5 and GLM (e.g., Qwen3.5-27B
rises from 0% to 22% on GSM8K), while gpt-oss remains near zero. Qwen3.5-9B starts with
higher baseline follow-up on GPQA Diamond (13.1%) and rises further with temperature.

use of LLMs in multi-turn conversation often requires user reactions as part of the training
(Wu et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2026; Buening et al., 2026) to enable better and more capable
models. Our work asks a complementary question to these works (see App. E for additional
discussion). We provide an evaluation measure of the interaction capabilities of LLMs.
This work makes the following contributions: (i) We formalize user-turn generation as a
probe of interaction awareness and jointly measure assistant accuracy, follow-up quality
in LLMs (§ 2). (ii) Across 11 models spanning 0.8B to 397B parameters, we demonstrate
interaction awareness is decoupled from task accuracy across model families (§ 3.1). (iii)
We show that this ability can be latent in a model’s generation distribution, surfaced with
higher temperature sampling, yet does not scale with model size even within a single
family

2   User Turn Generation

Effective conversation requires more than producing correct response for a given input.
It requires, in addition, anticipating the consequence to the response provided from the
other side. Recent work on multi-agent and agent to agent systems shows that such partner
models are necessary and only emerge when the training environment imposes sufficient
pressure for them to matter (Mon-Williams et al., 2025). To this end, we ask the question:

2
 Salesforce AI Research

even without explicitly optimizing for user-turn generation, can current LLMs generate user
turns that are grounded in the preceding assistant response?

2.1   Problem Formulation

Consider an LLM parameterized by θ, Mθ , post-trained with a chat template that defines
user and assistant role headers. Given a conversation context consisting of a user query q
and an assistant response a, we define user-turn generation as the sequence produced when
Mθ generates under the user role header, conditioned on the preceding context (Figure 1):
                                         u = Mθ ([q; a])                                       (1)
The question of study in this work is whether u constitutes a grounded follow-up to a, a
reaction that could arise from processing the content of the assistant response. A grounded
u is evidence that θ has learned to produce contextually appropriate user continuations; a
degenerate u indicates that, at least under the model’s modal continuation, the training has
not shaped the weights to generate plausible conversational consequences.
Thi
2.2   Measuring Interaction Awareness

We define a genuine follow-up as a generated user turn u whose content is grounded in the
context (q, a), i.e., u references, reacts to, or builds upon specific content in the conversation.
Conversely, a degenerate user turn is one that fails this grounding criterion: verbatim restate-
ment of q, continuation of the assistant response under the user role, emission of planning
artifacts, or generation of another task query. These failure modes indicate that the model
defaults to task-solving behavior without producing a contextually grounded user reaction.
We propose to measure interaction awareness through an LLM-based evaluator (Zheng
et al., 2023) that analyzes the full conversation trace. Given the query q, a, u, the evaluator
performs an assessment:
                         F OLLOW U P E VAL (q, a, u) = {rationale, ℓ, σ },                     (2)
where σ ∈ {0, 1} is a binary genuine-

2.3   Experimental Setup

We evaluate user-turn generation across 11 open-weight models, 5 benchmark datasets, and
2 held-out conversational datasets. We complement these with controlled perturbations and
collaboration-oriented post-training.

Figure 2: Controlled perturbation examples. Left: Truncation removes the end of the
assistant response, prompting the model to produce a reaction to complete the response.
Right: Appending a generic question elicits a grounded critique rather than a prompt
restatement. Assistant and user turns in the examples are generated by GLM-4.7. These
controls demonstrate that interaction awareness can surface in specific contexts.

Generation settings. In the self-generated setting, given a prompt q, we generate a from
Mθ and then u from the same model under the user role (Eq. 1). In the held-out setting,
used for post-training transfer checks and judge validation, we remove the final user turn
from multi-turn conversations and ask the model to generate under the user role. All
generation uses vLLM1 with modified chat templates that permit user-role continuation.
We primarily use temperature 0 (deterministic/greedy) generation; additional sampled
generations (T ∈ {0.3, 0.7, 1.0}) probe whether interaction awareness is present in a model’s
broader distribution (§3.3).

Datasets. We use 5 benchmark datasets where assistant behavior is independently ver-
ifiable, enabling joint measurement of task accuracy and interaction awareness: GSM8K
(Cobbe et al., 2021) (1319 examples, math reasoning), IFEval (Zhou et al., 2023) (541 exam-
ples) and IFBench (Pyatkin et al., 2025) (300 examples, instruction following), and GPQA
main (448 examples) and GPQA diamond (198 examples) (Rein et al., 2024) (expert QA).
On GSM8K we extract the final numeric answer; on GPQA the final letter, with extraction
failures counted as incorrect. Held-out datasets are HealthBench (Arora et al., 2025) (1470
examples) and a subset of Coval (100 examples) (Hitzig et al., 2026), where existing human
follow-ups provide a natural setup for interaction.

Models. We evaluate 11 open-weight models: eight Qwen3.5 models from 0.8B to 397B-
A17B (Qwen Team, 2026), two gpt-oss models (20B, 120B) (OpenAI, 2025), and GLM-4.7
(Zeng et al., 2025). The Qwen3.5 sweep provides within-family scaling analysis; cross-model
comparison controls for differences in training data and post-training recipes.

Controlled perturbations. If the genuine-followup rate measures something real about
interaction awareness, it should respond to causal manipulations of the assistant turn. We
design two perturbations as positive controls (Figure 2). Truncation removes max(25, ⌈0.25 ×
ntokens ⌉) tokens from the end of the assistant response before generating the user turn;
a model that attends to assistant-turn content should produce more genuine follow-ups

when the response is visibly incomplete. Explicit question appends a question sampled
from a pool of generic follow-ups (e.g., “What do you think?”, “Any questions?”) to the
assistant response, testing sensitivity to an overt conversational cue rather than an implicit
degradation.

Post-training interventions. To test whether interaction awareness is trainable, we apply
the CollabLLM recipe (Wu et al., 2025) to Qwen3.5-2B, which optimizes for multi-turn collab-
oration quality via forward-sampled conversation rewards. Training data comes from 200
level-5 MATH problems (Hendrycks et al., 2021), expanded into ≈ 5k multi-turn examples
(≈ 5 turns each). We train two variants for 1 epoch: (i) SFT with masked loss on assistant
turns, and (ii) online RL with PPO (Schulman et al., 2017) using multiturn rollouts and con-
versational rewards. Crucially, neither intervention explicitly trains for user-turn generation;
both target assistant response quality only. Any change in follow-up rate therefore reflects
an indirect effect of collaboration-aware training. Neither the evaluation benchmarks nor
held-out datasets appear in training, so gains reflect transfer.

3                                      Results

We present the results of our analysis in five parts: (i) task accuracy does not reflect follow-up
quality across model families (§3.1); (ii) temperature sampling reveals interaction awareness
is latent in the generation distribution while appearing absent with deterministic generation
(§3.3); (iii) a within-family deep dive shows interaction awareness does not scale with
model size (§3.2); (iv) controlled perturbations confirm the proposed metric (§3.4); and (v)
collaboration-oriented post-training can increase interaction awareness (§3.5).

3.1                                    Task Accuracy Does Not Predict Follow-Up Quality Across Families

If interaction awareness were simply a function of general capability, we would expect
models with higher task accuracy to also produce better follow-ups. We evaluate representa-
tive models from the Qwen3.5 and gpt-oss families alongside GLM-4.7 under deterministic

Figure 4: Qwen3.5 family: interaction awareness across temperature. Genuine follow-up
rate (%) vs. sampling temperature for all eight Qwen3.5 models on three datasets. At T =0
(greedy), follow-up is near zero for most models despite high task accuracy (Table 3). Higher
temperatures surface latent interaction awareness, but this does not scale monotonically
with model size: Qwen3.5-9B and Qwen3.5-27B often match or exceed larger models. MoE
variants (35B-A3B, 122B-A10B) consistently lag their dense counterparts.

generation (T =0). Figure 3 presents the results: task accuracy (top row) and followup rate
(bottom row) show distinctive behavior and remain largely uncorrelated.
gpt-oss models produce 17-21% follow-ups on GPQA Diamond despite lower answer
accuracy (61-67%) than GLM-4.7 (66.4% accuracy, 1.5% follow-up) or Qwen3.5-397B (86.1%
accuracy, 0.5% follow-up). Qwen3.5-397B-A17B follow up rate is strongest on IFBench (9.7%)
but weak on GPQA. Thus, different families develop interaction awareness on different
input contexts, and follow-up quality is not a monotone function of answer quality. This
provides deeper evidence for why LLMs struggle when repurposed as user proxies for self-
play or multi-agent interaction (Shekkizhar et al., 2025; Zhou et al., 2026): the disconnect is
rooted in absent interaction awareness, not insufficient task competence. Appendix C gives
representative traces for dif

3.2                              Within-Family Analysis: Model Size Does Not Predict Interaction Awareness

3.3                              Interaction Awareness Is Latent in the Distribution

The cross-family results above use deterministic generation (T =0), which probes only
the model’s most likely user continuation. A natural question is whether the near-zero
follow-up rates reflect a genuine absence of interaction awareness or whether the capability
exists in the distribution but is suppressed at the mode. We run a temperature sweep
(T ∈ {0, 0.3, 0.7, 1.0}) on six representative models across GSM8K, GPQA Diamond, and
IFBench (Figure 1, right).
The results reveal a clear separation between model families. Qwen3.5 and GLM show
strong temperature scaling: Qwen3.5-27B rises from 0% to 22% on GSM8K, from 1.5% to
35.9% on GPQA Diamond, and from 1% to 30.7% on IFBench. GLM-4.7 shows a similar
pattern, reaching 15.2% on GSM8K and 35.4% on GPQA Diamond at T =1.0. In contrast,
gpt-oss models demonstrate mixed response to sampling: gpt-oss-120b remains at 0.1%
on GSM8K even at T =1.

els correctly answer a GPQA chemistry question (Answer: D). Left: Qwen3.5-9B generates a

qualitatively different training outcome: these models have not learned interaction-aware
continuations for these contexts at any probability. This distinction between latent and
genuinely-absent interaction awareness motivates the next section on Qwen3.5 analysis.
To disentangle model size from training recipe, we examine the full Qwen3.5 family (0.8B
to 397B-A17B) under both deterministic and sampled generation. At T =0, task accuracy
scales reliably: GSM8K climbs from 41.6% (0.8B) to 96.8% (397B-A17B), GPQA Diamond
from 23.6% to 86.1%, and IFBench from 21.6% to 51.6%2 . Follow-up rates, however, stay
near zero: five of eight models produce 0.0% on GSM8K (Table 3). Results on IFEval and
GPQA Main are consistent (Appendix A).
The full temperature sweep (Figure 4) reveals that this non-scaling persists even when
interaction awareness is surfaced through sampling. On IFBench at T =1.0, th

3.4    Controlled Perturbations Confirm the Metric

Both perturbations move the metric in the expected direction for models that attend to
assistant-turn content, confirming that the genuine-followup rate measures something real
about interaction awareness.

Table 1: Controlled perturbations confirm the metric. Genuine-followup rate (%) under
two perturbations. Left: Truncating the assistant response increases follow-up for non-
Qwen models, confirming sensitivity to assistant-turn content. Right: Appending a generic
question (e.g., “What do you think?”) to the assistant response. Base: unperturbed follow-
up rate; +Question: follow-up rate after appending the question; % Changed: fraction of
user turns whose text differs from the unperturbed generation, measuring whether the
model attends to the perturbation at all. gpt-oss models respond most strongly to both
perturbations; Qwen models are largely insensitive. Additional results in Appendix A.

Truncation. Table 1 (Left) shows the effect of truncating assistant responses. On GSM8K,
where unperturbed follow-up is near zero, truncation sharply increases genuine follow-up
for GLM-4.7 (1.0% → 55.0%) and gpt-oss-120b (0.0% → 24.2%). The effect extends to GPQA
Diamond: GLM-4.7 rises from 2.0% to 39.4%, and gpt-oss-120b from 20.7% to 65.7%. Qwen
models are notably less sensitive: Qwen3.5-27B shows no change on GSM8K and remains
flat on GPQA Diamond. This family-level asymmetry suggests qualitatively different failure
modes: Qwen defaults to prompt restatement regardless of assistant content, while gpt-oss
and GLM attend to the assistant turn even when their continuation is not a follow-up.

Explicit question. Table 1 (Right) shows the complementary perturbation on the
instruction-following datasets. gpt-oss-120b shows the strongest response: genuine
follow-up jumps from 1.3% to 25.7% on IFBench, with 99.0% of user turns changing
text. gpt-oss-20b follows at smaller magnitude (1.0% → 6.0%). GLM-4.7 changes 99%
of its user turns but genuine follow-up barely increases (4.7% → 5.0% on IFBench).
Qwen models again show low sensitivity: Qwen3.5-27B changes only 6.3% of turns, and
Qwen3.5-397B-A17B decreases in genuine follow-up (7.7% → 4.7% on IFBench), suggesting
the perturbation disrupts its existing follow-up behavior rather than eliciting new responses.
Across both perturbations, gpt-oss models are the most responsive: they attend to assistant-
turn content and convert that attention into genuine follow-ups. GLM-4.7 attends strongly
(high changed rates) but does not proportion
3.5    Post-Training Can Move Interaction Awareness

The preceding results establish that interaction awareness is largely absent from current
models under default generation. A natural question is whether it can be moved through
training. We apply collaboration-oriented post-training to Qwen3.5-2B (§2.3) and find that
both SFT and RL raise the genuine-followup rate (Table 2), despite no overlap between the
training data and the evaluation datasets in our results.
Supervised finetuning (SFT) produces large gains: follow-up rises from 1.0-2.0% to 46-48%
on verifiable benchmarks and from 36.7% to 54.4% on held-out HealthBench, at the cost of
GSM8K accuracy (62.9% → 40.3%), likely due to forgetting/overfitting to ≈ 5k multi-turn
examples. The reinforcement learning (RL) variant avoids this tradeoff, preserving task
accuracy (67.4%) while giving moderate follow-up gains across all datasets. Recall that
neither intervention explicitly trains fo

Table 2: Post-training on Qwen3.5-2B. GSM8K accuracy for reference. Followup rates on
IFBench, GPQA Diamond and held-out conversational datasets (HealthBench, Coval).

These preliminary results suggest the follow-up eval metric can serve as an indicator of
collaboration quality and post-training design on this dimension.

4   Discussion and Conclusion

This work formalizes user-turn generation as a probe of interaction awareness in LLMs.
Across 11 open-weight models spanning Qwen3.5, gpt-oss families and GLM-4.7, evaluated
on 5 benchmark datasets, we demonstrate that interaction awareness is largely decoupled
from task performance. Within Qwen3.5, accuracy scales from 41.6% to 96.8% on GSM8K
while grounded follow-up rate stays near zero at every scale. This gap is not uniform:
gpt-oss shows relatively higher follow up on GPQA, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B on IFBench, and
Qwen3.5-9B as a within-family outlier. Controlled perturbations and collaboration-oriented
post-training both move the metric in interpretable directions, confirming that interaction
awareness is latent, measurable, and can be increased through targeted training. Both
SFT and RL on Qwen3.5-2B raise follow-up rates across datasets despite neither explicitly
training for user-turn

C    Representative Generation Examples

We present one dominant failure mode and one genuine follow-up per model family,
illustrating the distinct failure profiles described in Appendix B. Each example abridges the
original prompt and shows the assistant excerpt and generated user turn.

Qwen3.5-27B: prompt restatement (GSM8K).

Task (abridged). “John arm wrestles 20 people. He beats 80%. How many did he
        lose to?”
        Assistant excerpt. “Step 1: 80% of 20 = 16 wins. Step 2: 20 − 16 = 4 losses.
        Answer: 4”
        Generated user turn. Verbatim copy of the original prompt, character-for-character.
        Judge. previous turn restate; nongenuine.

This is the modal Qwen failure (78.5% of all generations). The model ignores the assistant
response entirely and restarts the conversation. On GSM8K, Qwen3.5-27B produces 100%
restatement.

Qwen3.5-9B: grounded challenge (GPQA Diamond).
        Task (abridged). Astrophysics question about relativistic travel from the Large
        Magellanic Cloud.
        Assistant excerpt. “. . . 22 + 81.6 = 103.6 years. Since 103.6 ¡ 150, the astronaut
        survives . . . Answer: C”
        Generated user turn. “In the previous question, the distance from the Large
        Magellanic Cloud to Earth was not explicitly stated in the problem description.
        How did you determine the distance used in your calculation?”
        Judge. plausible followup; genuine.

A natural clarification request grounded in a specific gap in the assistant’s reasoning.
Qwen3.5-9B produces 13.1% genuine follow-ups on GPQA Diamond versus near-zero for
most other Qwen sizes.

gpt-oss-120b: internal verification leak (GSM8K).
        Task (abridged). “A robe takes 2 bolts of blue fiber and half that much white fiber.
        How many bolts in total?”
        Assistant excerpt. “Blue fiber = 2 bolts. White fiber = half of 2 = 1 bolt. Total = 3.
        Answer: 3”
        Generated user turn. <|channel|>analysis<|message|> “We need to ensure final
        answer line exactly ‘Answer: ¡final answer¿’ with no extra spaces . . . The solution
        must be at most 6 short steps, we have 3 steps, fine . . . ”
        Judge. assistant turn restate; nongenuine.

The gpt-oss dominant failure (70.2%): the model emits internal chain-of-thought verification
under the user role, complete with a <|channel|>analysis prefix that is clearly model
scaffolding, not a human follow-up.

gpt-oss-120b: grounded continuation (GPQA Diamond).
        Task (abridged). Chemistry question about optically active compounds.
        Assistant excerpt. “We need to count chiral compounds. Evaluate each: (Z)-1-
        chloro-2-methylbut-1-ene . . . ”
        Generated user turn. <|channel|>analysis<|message|> “We need to decide if
        (1s,3s,5s)-cyclohexane-1,3,5-triol is chiral. Let’s draw chair conformation . . . The
        stereochemistry S at each carbon defines orientation . . . ”
        Judge. plausible followup; genuine.

Despite retaining the <|channel|>analysis framing, this example drills into a specific sub-
problem that the assistant left partially unresolved, a genuine continuation rather than mere
verification.

GLM-4.7: planning as user (IFEval).
        Task (abridged). “Write a resume for a fresh high school graduate with at least 12
        placeholders in square brackets.”
        Assistant excerpt. “[Name] [Address] [Phone Number] [Email Address] OBJEC-
        TIVE Motivated and reliable high school graduate . . . ”
        Generated user turn. “Here’s a thinking process that leads to the suggested resume:
        1. Analyze the Request: Target Audience: Fresh high school graduate. Constraint:
        Must include at least 12 placeholders . . . 2. Determine the Structure . . . ”
        Judge. meta planning; nongenuine.

GLM’s dominant failure (43%): the model generates assistant-style reasoning under the user
role, beginning with “Here’s a thinking process.” It attends to the role-header change but
fails to adopt a user perspective.

GLM-4.7: natural revision request (IFEval).
        Task (abridged). “Write two different cover letters for a software engineer role at
        Google—a concise version and a long version.”

These examples illustrate the family-specific failure profiles from Table 6: Qwen restates
the prompt, gpt-oss leaks internal verification, and GLM emits planning text. Genuine
follow-ups, when they occur, are grounded in specific content of the assistant response, such
as, clarification requests, factual challenges, or revision instructions.

D     LLM Judge Validation and Human Annotation

To validate the reliability of our LLM-based evaluation system, we conducted both same-
family judge comparisons and a blinded manual review study with human annotators.
This section details the judge selection rationale, cross-judge agreement, human annotation
protocol, and correlation analysis.

D.1   Same-Family Judge Validation

Paper-facing tables in this draft use exact-match preprocessing followed by a gpt-5.4-mini
judge. We switched from the earlier gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17 operational judge because
same-family judge validation and two blinded human packets both favored mini. The
relevant question here is therefore not cross-family agreement, but how much the older
nano results contract under mini and whether mini tracks full gpt-5.4. We report three
same-family checks. First, we rerun the entire real-follow-up panel with gpt-5.4-mini.
Second, we keep a prevalence-preserving random slice with all Coval examples plus 300
random HealthBench examples per model. Third, we keep a balanced hard-case slice with
all Coval examples plus 100 HealthBench examples per model, balanced by the nano judge’s
genuine-versus-nongenuine decision.
Table 7 gives a more useful signal than a cross-family comparison would. On the ful

D.2    Manual Review Methodology

To validate the reliability of our LLM-based evaluation system beyond judge-to-judge
comparison, we conducted a blinded manual review study with human annotators.

Annotation protocol. We constructed multiple 100-example annotation packets sampled
from the full evaluation panel. Each packet was drawn as a random sample from the mix
of all datasets used in the paper. We constructed two types of packets: hard-case packets
built from nano versus gpt-5.4 disagreements plus judge-agree genuine and judge-agree
nongenuine examples, and natural-prevalence packets sampled proportionally from the full
nano versus mini panel. Packets were distributed to peer AI researchers with expertise in
LLMs; we obtained 5 completed annotation returns.
Each conversation was presented through a standardized annotation interface (Figure 6)
showing the conversation context and the generated user turn. Annotators did not see
the model identity, dataset metadata, reference user turn, or any judge label or rationale,
ensuring that human labels were not biased by automated class

Correlation analysis. We report human-vs-judge agreement at the binary genuine-
followup level, as this is the primary metric used throughout the paper. We did not compute
inter-annotator agreement, as our focus was on coverage across packets and correlation
with the LLM judge rather than cross-annotator reliability.
On the hard packet, gpt-5.4-mini aligned best with blinded human annotators: 87.0%
genuine-followup agreement with κ = 0.688, versus 67.0% / 0.255 for nano and 77.0%
/ 0.481 for full gpt-5.4. On the natural-prevalence packet, mini again beats nano: 94.0%
genuine-followup agreement with κ = 0.766, versus 79.0% / 0.354. Pooled across both
blinded packets, mini reaches 90.5% genuine-followup agreement with κ = 0.726, versus
73.0% / 0.311 for nano. Primary-label agreement remains materially lower (58.5% with
κ = 0.481 for pooled mini), so the human evidence supports the binary j

E     Related Work

Theory of mind in LLMs. Evaluating whether LLMs can attribute beliefs and intentions
to others has produced a consistent finding: models pass explicit belief-attribution probes
but fail when the same reasoning must be expressed behaviorally (Sap et al., 2022; Kim
et al., 2023; Ma et al., 2023). Gu et al. (2024) make this precise: frontier models achieve
> 95% on mental-state inference yet drop below 50% on behavior prediction and below

17
 Salesforce AI Research

Figure 6: Manual review interface. Screenshot of the annotation tool used for LLM judge
validation. The interface presents the conversation context and the generated user turn to
annotate, along with the primary-label inventory and a binary genuine-followup decision.
Model identity, dataset metadata, and judge labels are hidden from annotators.

20% on judgment. This illustrates “knowing versus applying” dissociation. Our accuracy
vs followup gap is structurally analogous, but where SimpleToM probes reasoning about
another agent’s mental state (third-person inference), we probe whether a model encodes
the consequence of its own response (first-person behavioral readout). Both converge on
the same conclusion: solving the immediate task does not guarantee modeling what comes
next.

User simulation. User simulation has a long history in dialogue systems (Schatzmann
et al., 2007; Shi et al., 2019), and same-model self-play has been used for data generation and
evaluation (Ghandeharioun et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2023). Recent work builds dedicated user
proxies: Naous et al. (2026) train user models by flipping dialogue turns, Dou et al. (2025)
benchmark simulator reliability, Hathidara et al. (2026) evaluate human-likeness, and Zhou
et al. (2026) show that current simulators are overly cooperative and inflate agent scores.

18
 Salesforce AI Research

Our goal is different: we do not build a reusable user proxy but use user-turn generation as
a probe of the assistant model’s own weights. A high-quality external simulator does not
imply that the assistant itself possesses interaction awareness. The failure modes we catalog
(prompt restatement, meta-planning, identity leakage) characterize the same behaviors
Zhou et al. (2026) observe when assistant models are repurposed as user proxies without
dedicated training.

Multi-turn optimization and interaction-aware training. Standard post-training opti-
mizes the current response via single-turn preferences or rewards (Ouyang et al., 2022;
Rafailov et al., 2023). Recent work extends the training signal beyond the current turn: Wu
et al. (2025) optimize for future collaboration quality via forward-sampled multiturn rollouts,
Buening et al. (2026) use post-action user states as supervision, ArCHer (Zhou et al., 2024)
applies hierarchical multi-turn RL, and Wang et al. (2026) frame every agent interaction as
producing a “next-state signal” that encodes evaluative and directive information for online
learning. In multi-agent RL, Mon-Williams et al. (2025) show that partner models emerge in
recurrent agents but only when the environment provides influence over partner behavior,
a conditional emergence paralleled by our perturbation results (§3.4), where inte

Interactive benchmarks. τ 2 -Bench (Barres et al., 2025), and CRMArena (Huang et al.,
2025) evaluate agents in tool-mediated settings with simulated users and task-level rewards,
embedding consequence modeling in richer environments. Proactive dialogue work (Deng
et al., 2023; Andukuri et al., 2024; Faltings et al., 2023) similarly argues that agents must
model the interaction trajectory. Our probe is narrower and this simplicity enables attribu-
tion of differences to model weights and training rather than environment confounds. A
model that fails our probe lacks the interaction awareness needed for the richer settings
these benchmarks target.

F   Continued Discussion

A natural concern is whether the near-zero follow-up rates on benchmark datasets reflect
an absence of interaction awareness or are better explained by artifacts of the training
distribution and chat template design. We consider three alternative explanations and
discuss the evidence for and against each.

Single-turn training data. Models post-trained predominantly on single-turn (query,
response) pairs will have learned that the most likely token sequence under the user role
is a new query, not a follow-up to the preceding response. Under this explanation, the
low follow-up rate is a distributional artifact rather than evidence about what the model’s
representations encode. We view this as complementary to our framing: if the training
data did not include multi-turn continuations, then the model’s training did not instill
interaction awareness, which is precisely the gap we identify. The post-training results (§3.5)
directly test this: collaboration-oriented training on multi-turn data raises follow-up rates,
confirming that the training distribution is a causal factor.

Chat template conventions. The <|im end|> token after the assistant turn may function
as a strong “conversation complete” signal in some training pipelines, biasing the model
toward starting a new exchange rather than continuing the current one. If this were the
sole explanation, we would expect all models using the same chat template to show similar
follow-up rates. However, within the Qwen3.5 family (which shares a template), follow-up
rates vary from 0.0% to 13.1% across model sizes and datasets. Cross-family, gpt-oss models

19
 Salesforce AI Research

produce substantially higher follow-up than Qwen models of comparable or larger size
on GPQA, despite all models using role-delimited chat formats. The perturbation results
further argue against a purely template-level explanation: truncation and explicit-question
perturbations change gpt-oss and GLM follow-up rates substantially while leaving Qwen
largely unaffected (§3.4).

Role-header semantics. Different model families may have been trained with different
conventions for what follows a user-role header (a new independent query versus a con-
tinuation of the same conversation). We do not have access to the post-training data for
any of the evaluated models, so we cannot rule this out entirely. However, we control for it
indirectly through the held-out evaluation setting (§2.3), where the model generates a user
turn given a real multi-turn conversation context. On HealthBench and Coval, follow-up
rates are substantially higher (19–48%) than on benchmark datasets, suggesting that when
the conversational context is richer, even models with low benchmark follow-up rates can
produce grounded continuations. This is consistent with interaction awareness being latent
but context-dependent, rather than absent.

Behavioral versus representational claims. We emphasize that our probe measures the
model’s behavioral output under the user role, not its internal representations. A model
could in principle encode rich conversational structure internally while still producing
restatement as the modal continuation, if its training objective did not incentivize expressing
that structure under the user role. Representation-level analyses (e.g., probing internal
activations for next-turn content) would complement the behavioral probe we propose. Our
claims are therefore about what the model produces, conditioned on the experimental setup,
rather than about the totality of what the model knows. We view this as a feature of the
probe rather than a limitation: deployment-relevant interaction awareness must ultimately
manifest in generated text, not only in latent representations.

G    LLM Usage

For this paper, we made use of LLM tools for three specific purposes: (i) writing code, in
particular, for overseeing GPU runs and plotting results from jsonl and csv logs, (ii) review
and polish writing, such as, making content concise, checking for statements and claims
that were not backed by results in the paper, and (iii) searching for references related to
a particular topic. In all scenarios, the responses generated by the LLM were verified for
accuracy by the authors, to the best of their knowledge.
